title: Oat flapjacks
subtitle: Golden syrup oat bars, chewy or crisp depending on how long they bake
excerpt: Sixteen flapjacks from a 20 × 20 cm tin: rolled oats bound with butter, golden syrup, and muscovado sugar, pressed into the tin and baked until golden at the edges. Short baking gives chewy bars; an extra 5 minutes gives a crisper snap. Score while warm, cool completely before cutting.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the oaten biscuit section documents a baked oat mixture bound with butter and treacle or syrup, pressed into a tin and cut after baking. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the oat cake chapter documents the use of golden syrup as a binding agent in baked oat mixtures. The British flapjack in its standard form of rolled oats, butter, sugar, and golden syrup is documented across domestic baking literature from the late Victorian period onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cut bars in a bag, layered with baking paper. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.batchNotes: Use a 20 × 30 cm tin for a larger batch.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Flapjacks keep in an airtight tin for up to 2 weeks. They chew better on days 2 and 3 once the syrup has settled.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; 20–22 minutes for chewy, 25–27 minutes for crisp

glossaryTerms.definition: Cutting shallow lines through a baked traybake into portion shapes while it is still warm and soft from the oven, then allowing it to cool completely before cutting all the way through and removing from the tin. Trying to cut flapjacks cold cracks them. Scoring while warm marks the portions cleanly and allows the cuts to deepen as the flapjack sets, so the final cut requires minimal pressure.

content.text: A flapjack has four ingredients. The ratio is easy to remember and the method takes 10 minutes. The only decision is texture: bake for 20 minutes and the bars are chewy; bake for 25 and they are crispier and snap more cleanly. Either way,
— flapjacks crack if you try to cut them cold.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Grease and line a 20 × 20 cm tin. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{light-muscovado-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together in a medium saucepan over low heat, stirring until the butter is melted and the sugar has dissolved — do not boil. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{rolled-oats}} until all the oats are coated.

Tip the oat mixture into the prepared tin. Press down firmly and evenly with the back of a spoon or damp hands — pack the oats as densely as possible or the bars will crumble when cut. Bake for 20–22 minutes for chewy, or 25–27 minutes for a crispier result. The edges should be golden-brown and the surface should look dry. The centre will still look slightly liquid through the oats.

Remove from the oven and immediately score into 16 bars with a sharp knife (4 cuts one way and 3 cuts the other) pressing through the surface but not lifting the bars. Leave to cool completely in the tin, at least 30 minutes, before cutting all the way through and removing. The bars will firm up significantly as they cool. Store in an airtight tin.
